text: A retrieval team is called to a district general hospital for a 34-year-old woman at 38 weeks gestation who collapsed during a routine antenatal review. She is pulseless. The midwife has commenced CPR. The arrest occurred 3 minutes ago. What is the recommended maximum time from maternal cardiac arrest to delivery of the foetus if resuscitation is unsuccessful?

options.A: 10 minutes
options.B: 5 minutes
options.C: 15 minutes
options.D: 20 minutes
options.E: 4 minutes

explanation:
Perimortem Caesarean section (PMCS) — also termed Resuscitative Hysterotomy — should be performed within 5 minutes of maternal cardiac arrest if ROSC has not been achieved. This target is established by the Resuscitation Council UK (RCUK) Advanced Life Support guidelines (2021), RCOG guidelines, and the Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine. The rationale is dual: (1) relieving aortocaval compression from the gravid uterus (which reduces the effectiveness of CPR by up to 30%) significantly improves chances of maternal ROSC; (2) foetal hypoxic-ischaemic injury begins within minutes of maternal arrest. The procedure should not be delayed waiting for surgical equipment or theatre — a scalpel at the bedside is sufficient. After 20 weeks gestation, the uterus is large enough to cause significant aortocaval compression during CPR. The RCUK guidelines state: "Initiate PMCS immediately if there is no response to initial resuscitation." Survival rates are highest when delivery occurs within 5 minutes, though cases of intact survival at longer intervals exist. This is a critical knowledge point for retrieval physicians who may arrive at a scene where this decision must be made.
________________________________________

text: A 29-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation is transferred from a peripheral maternity unit with eclampsi

options.A: She is post-ictal, GCS 11, BP 180/112 mmHg, on a magnesium sulphate infusion at 1g/hour. En route, she has a second tonic-clonic seizure. What is the correct immediate pharmacological response? A. Administer IV diazepam 10 mg
options.B: Administer a further IV bolus of magnesium sulphate 2g over 5 minutes
options.C: Stop the magnesium sulphate infusion and start IV phenytoin
options.D: Administer IV lorazepam 4 mg
options.E: What is the correct immediate pharmacological response? A. Administer IV diazepam 10 mg B. Administer a further IV bolus of magnesium sulphate 2g over 5 minutes C. Stop the magnesium sulphate infusion and start IV phenytoin D. Administer IV lorazepam 4 mg E. Administer IM morphine 10 mg for pain management

explanation:
The first-line treatment for eclamptic seizures, and for recurrent seizures in a patient already on magnesium sulphate maintenance infusion, is an additional IV bolus of magnesium sulphate 2g given slowly over 5 minutes. This is established in NICE guideline NG133 (Hypertension in Pregnancy, 2019), RCOG Green-top Guideline 10A (The Management of Severe Pre-eclampsia/Eclampsia), and the Collaborative Eclampsia Trial. The maintenance infusion should be continued. Benzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam — options A and D) are second-line and are associated with neonatal respiratory depression; they should only be used if magnesium fails to control seizures. Phenytoin (option C) is no longer recommended for eclampsia management. If signs of magnesium toxicity are present (loss of patellar reflexes, respiratory depression, cardiac arrhythmia), magnesium should be stopped and calcium gluconate 10 ml of 10% solution given IV as the antidote. In the retrieval context, it is essential to monitor reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output (as renal clearance determines magnesium excretion) throughout the transfer.
________________________________________

text: A 26-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks is transferred from a rural maternity unit to a tertiary perinatal centre. She is in suspected preterm labour with regular contractions and a cervix 3 cm dilated. Which of the following interventions is most appropriate before departure to improve foetal outcomes if delivery occurs?

options.A: IV atosiban tocolysis to halt labour entirely
options.B: IM betamethasone 11.4 mg to promote foetal lung maturity
options.C: Oral nifedipine as tocolysis to allow 48 hours of steroid effect
options.D: Which of the following interventions is most appropriate before departure to improve foetal outcomes if delivery occurs? A. IV atosiban tocolysis to halt labour entirely B. IM betamethasone 11.4 mg to promote foetal lung maturity C. Oral nifedipine as tocolysis to allow 48 hours of steroid effect D. CTG monitoring throughout transfer
options.E: She is in suspected preterm labour with regular contractions and a cervix 3 cm dilated. Which of the following interventions is most appropriate before departure to improve foetal outcomes if delivery occurs? A. IV atosiban tocolysis to halt labour entirely B. IM betamethasone 11.4 mg to promote foetal lung maturity C. Oral nifedipine as tocolysis to allow 48 hours of steroid effect D. CTG monitoring throughout transfer E. IV oxytocin to expedite delivery before transfer

explanation:
The rationale for both interventions is interlinked. Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, repeated after 24 hours — option B alone is insufficient as a single dose provides incomplete coverage) reduce the incidence of respiratory distress syndrome, intraventricular haemorrhage, and neonatal death in preterm infants. Tocolysis buys time for the steroids to take effect — the full benefit requires at least 24–48 hours. NICE guideline NG25 (Preterm Labour and Birth) recommends nifedipine as the preferred tocolytic agent, with the regimen: 20 mg orally (chewed with orange juice), repeated at 20 and 40 minutes, then 20 mg three times daily for up to 48 hours. Option B alone is incomplete without tocolysis in a patient contracting at 3 cm dilation. Option A (atosiban) is an alternative tocolytic but is not the preferred first-line agent per NICE. CTG monitoring (option D) is not recommended during transfer as there is no capacity to act on abnormalities found en route — RCOG and the Oxford Specialist Handbook both advise against routine intrapartum CTG during transfer. Option E is the opposite of the correct management goal.
________________________________________

text: When positioning a conscious pregnant woman beyond 20 weeks gestation for transfer in the supine position on a stretcher, what is the primary reason for applying a left lateral tilt of 15–30°?

options.A: To improve maternal respiratory mechanics by reducing diaphragmatic compression
options.B: To reduce the risk of aspiration by facilitating gastric emptying
options.C: To relieve aortocaval compression by the gravid uterus, maintaining cardiac output
options.D: To improve foetal oxygenation by increasing placental blood flow through gravity
options.E: To prevent supine hypotensive syndrome caused by progesterone-induced vasodilation

explanation:
The gravid uterus beyond approximately 20 weeks gestation compresses both the inferior vena cava (reducing venous return and cardiac preload) and the descending aorta (reducing uterine and placental perfusion pressure) when the patient is supine. This can reduce maternal cardiac output by up to 30% and cause foetal hypoxia. Left lateral tilt (a wedge or manual displacement to the left) displaces the uterus off these vessels, restoring near-normal haemodynamics. This is a cornerstone principle of obstetric resuscitation and retrieval, stated explicitly in RCUK ALS guidelines (2021), RCOG guidance, and the Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine ("Avoid aortocaval compression in pregnant women over 20 weeks' gestation"). Options A and D may be minor secondary benefits but are not the primary rationale. Option B is incorrect — left tilt does not facilitate gastric emptying. Option E identifies the correct consequence (supine hypotension) but the mechanism cited (progesterone-induced vasodilation) is incorrect — the primary cause is mechanical aortocaval compression.
________________________________________

text: A 31-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation is being retrieved with a suspected placental abruption. She is haemodynamically stable with moderate vaginal bleeding. She is Rh-negative. Which of the following should be performed at the referring hospital before departure?

options.A: Administration of IV Rh immunoglobulin 1500 IU
options.B: Kleihauer-Betke test and administration of anti-D immunoglobulin, with dose guided by the result
options.C: Cross-matching of 6 units of packed red blood cells and emergency obstetric review
options.D: Emergency Caesarean section to prevent further abruption
options.E: Which of the following should be performed at the referring hospital before departure? A. Administration of IV Rh immunoglobulin 1500 IU B. Kleihauer-Betke test and administration of anti-D immunoglobulin, with dose guided by the result C. Cross-matching of 6 units of packed red blood cells and emergency obstetric review D. Emergency Caesarean section to prevent further abruption E. Administration of misoprostol 800 mcg rectally

explanation:
In a Rh-negative woman with suspected placental abruption (a sensitising event causing potential foeto-maternal haemorrhage), anti-D immunoglobulin must be administered to prevent Rh sensitisation — which could cause haemolytic disease of the foetus and newborn (HDFN) in future pregnancies. The Kleihauer-Betke test (or equivalent flow cytometry) quantifies the degree of foeto-maternal haemorrhage and determines whether the standard prophylactic dose (500 IU for >20 weeks gestation per RCOG guideline) is adequate, or whether a higher dose is required. This is specified in RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 22 (The Use of Anti-D Immunoglobulin for Rhesus D Prophylaxis) and the Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine. Option A offers a fixed dose without considering the degree of haemorrhage. Cross-matching blood (option C) is appropriate for the haemodynamically unstable patient but doesn't address the Rh issue. Emergency Caesarean section (option D) is not indicated in a haemodynamically stable patient with moderate bleeding. Misoprostol (option E) is a uterotonic indicated for PPH or abortion, not for placental abruption management.
________________________________________

text: A 4-year-old child (weight 18 kg) is referred for retrieval with bronchiolitis and respiratory failure requiring intubation. Which endotracheal tube size and depth of insertion (at lips) is most appropriate?

options.A: Size 4.0 uncuffed ETT, inserted to 14 cm
options.B: Size 5.0 uncuffed ETT, inserted to 17 cm
options.C: Size 4.5 cuffed ETT, inserted to 14 cm
options.D: Size 3.5 uncuffed ETT, inserted to 12 cm
options.E: Size 5.0 cuffed ETT, inserted to 15 cm

explanation:
The standard formula for estimating paediatric ETT size is: Internal diameter (mm) = (age/4) + 4 for uncuffed tubes. For a 4-year-old: (4/4) + 4 = 5.0 mm uncuffed. However, for cuffed tubes (now preferred even in younger children per recent evidence and PALS guidelines): size = (age/4) + 3.5 = 4.5 mm cuffed. Current paediatric anaesthesia guidance (APLS, PALS, Royal College of Paediatrics and Child Health) recommends cuffed ETTs as they reduce the need for tube changes and better control airway seal during transport. Depth of insertion formula: (age/2) + 12 = (4/2) + 12 = 14 cm at the lips. In the retrieval context, using a cuffed tube is particularly recommended as it prevents air leaks during positive pressure ventilation and maintains monitoring accuracy (capnography). Options should always be verified clinically — good bilateral chest rise, equal air entry, and ETCO₂ waveform confirmation are essential. The Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine and APLS guidelines both support the use of cuffed ETTs for paediatric retrieval, with cuff inflation to achieve a seal at <20 cmH₂O pressure.
________________________________________

text: A 6-month-old infant (weight 7 kg) is retrieved with meningococcal septicaemia and septic shock. She has failed peripheral IV access after three attempts. What is the most appropriate next step for vascular access?

options.A: Further peripheral cannulation attempts, targeting antecubital fossa
options.B: Intraosseous access using an EZ-IO drill at the proximal tibia
options.C: Femoral venous cutdown
options.D: Umbilical venous catheterisation
options.E: Central venous line insertion at the right internal jugular vein

explanation:
Intraosseous (IO) access is the recommended rescue route for vascular access in critically ill children when peripheral IV access fails after two to three attempts, as per APLS (Advanced Paediatric Life Support), RCUK Paediatric ALS guidelines, and JRCALC. IO access via the proximal tibia (preferred site in infants and young children: 1–2 cm below the tibial tuberosity, medial flat surface) can be achieved in seconds using an EZ-IO drill and delivers fluids, blood products, and medications at rates comparable to peripheral IV. All standard resuscitation drugs, including antibiotics, can be administered via IO. In meningococcal septicaemia, IV ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg (max 2g) and fluid resuscitation must not be delayed — IO access enables this immediately. Femoral venous cutdown (option C) is a surgical technique appropriate only when all other options have failed. Umbilical venous catheterisation (option D) is a neonatal technique not applicable at 6 months. Internal jugular CVC (option E) is an advanced procedure with higher complication risk and should not precede IO in this acute scenario. NICE, RCPCH, and the Oxford Specialist Handbook all recommend IO access as a primary rescue strategy.
________________________________________

text: A 2-year-old child (weight 12 kg) with bacterial meningitis and septic shock requires fluid resuscitation before retrieval. Applying current NICE and RCPCH guidance for paediatric septic shock, what is the appropriate initial fluid bolus?

options.A: 20 ml/kg 0.9% sodium chloride as fast as possible
options.B: 10 ml/kg Hartmann's solution over 15 minutes, reassessing after each bolus
options.C: 30 ml/kg Hartmann's solution over 30 minutes
options.D: 10 ml/kg 4.5% human albumin solution
options.E: 5 ml/kg 0.9% sodium chloride over 10 minutes

explanation:
This question reflects an important shift in paediatric fluid resuscitation guidance following the FEAST trial (2011), which demonstrated harm from bolus fluid resuscitation in African children with severe febrile illness. Subsequent UK and international guidelines have been updated significantly. NICE CG102 (Bacterial meningitis and meningococcal disease) was updated in 2023, and current RCPCH and Paediatric Advanced Life Support guidance now recommends: initial fluid bolus of 10 ml/kg (not 20 ml/kg) of isotonic crystalloid (normal saline or Hartmann's), given over 15 minutes, with reassessment after each bolus. This is repeated as needed. The previous standard of 20 ml/kg rapidly has been replaced by smaller boluses with close reassessment — monitoring for signs of fluid overload (hepatomegaly, worsening respiratory status). Option A (20 ml/kg rapidly) is the older approach now superseded. Option C (30 ml/kg) is excessive and carries harm risk. Human albumin solution (option D) is not first-line. Option E is too small. This change in guidance is clinically significant and commonly tested.
________________________________________

text: During retrieval of a ventilated 3-year-old child (weight 14 kg) with viral encephalitis, the transport ventilator alarms repeatedly with "low tidal volume." The child is sedated but making respiratory efforts against the ventilator. What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.A: Increase tidal volume setting to overcome the child's respiratory efforts
options.B: Increase the respiratory rate to suppress the child's drive
options.C: Administer a bolus of rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg IV to facilitate synchrony
options.D: Administer additional sedation (
options.E: " The child is sedated but making respiratory efforts against the ventilator. What is the most appropriate immediate management? A. Increase tidal volume setting to overcome the child's respiratory efforts B. Increase the respiratory rate to suppress the child's drive C. Administer a bolus of rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg IV to facilitate synchrony D. Administer additional sedation (e.g. midazolam 0.1 mg/kg IV) and reassess E. Switch to manual ventilation until the child stops triggering

explanation:
Patient-ventilator dyssynchrony (the patient actively breathing against the ventilator) is a common problem during paediatric retrieval and leads to ineffective ventilation, increased work of breathing, and in TBI/encephalitis, can cause dangerous surges in ICP. The first-line response is to optimise sedation, not immediately to paralise. Additional sedation (midazolam, fentanyl, or propofol as appropriate) addresses the underlying problem of inadequate sedation. In a child with encephalitis, maintaining deep sedation during transfer is important for airway protection and preventing secondary brain injury. If sedation alone is insufficient, then neuromuscular blockade may be added — rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg (option C) would achieve this, but is not the first step before optimising sedation. Simply increasing tidal volume (option A) risks volutrauma. Increasing respiratory rate (option B) does not address the fundamental issue of patient effort. Manual ventilation (option E) may transiently improve synchrony but is not a sustainable solution and doesn't address the cause. APLS, RCPCH guidelines, and the Oxford Specialist Handbook all emphasise adequate sedation as the foundation of safe paediatric ventilation during transfer.
________________________________________

text: A 7-year-old child (weight 23 kg) is retrieved following status epilepticus. She received IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg twice without effect, then IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg, which also failed to terminate the seizures. She continues to seize. She is not yet intubated. What is the next most appropriate step?

options.A: Administer IV sodium valproate 40 mg/kg over 15 minutes
options.B: Administer IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes
options.C: Administer IM ketamine 4 mg/kg as an alternative anticonvulsant
options.D: What is the next most appropriate step? A. Administer IV sodium valproate 40 mg/kg over 15 minutes B. Administer IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes C. Administer IM ketamine 4 mg/kg as an alternative anticonvulsant D. Proceed to RSI with thiopentone for intubation and general anaesthetic control of seizures
options.E: She is not yet intubated. What is the next most appropriate step? A. Administer IV sodium valproate 40 mg/kg over 15 minutes B. Administer IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes C. Administer IM ketamine 4 mg/kg as an alternative anticonvulsant D. Proceed to RSI with thiopentone for intubation and general anaesthetic control of seizures E. Administer IV midazolam infusion at 0.1 mg/kg/hour

explanation:
This child has refractory status epilepticus (RSE) — defined as seizures continuing despite two appropriately dosed first-line agents (benzodiazepines) and one second-line agent (levetiracetam). The RCPCH and APLS guidelines define third-line treatment as requiring anaesthetic management: RSI with thiopentone (or propofol, or midazolam infusion in an intubated patient), with ventilation. Continuing to add further anticonvulsant agents without securing the airway risks respiratory depression, aspiration, and failure to control the seizure — prolonged seizures cause escalating neurological injury. Thiopentone as the induction agent for RSI simultaneously serves as an anticonvulsant via GABA-A receptor enhancement and NMDA receptor inhibition, and is the recommended agent for third-line management of RSE in the APLS and NICE CG137 guidelines. IV sodium valproate (option A) is an alternative second-line agent but this child has already failed a second-line agent — it is not the next step. Phenytoin (option B) is another second-line option but same reasoning applies. IM ketamine (option C) has no established role in seizure termination. Midazolam infusion (option E) requires an intubated patient. In the retrieval context, intubation before departure is essential.
________________________________________

text: A 5-year-old child is retrieved with epiglottitis. On examination, the child is sitting forward, drooling, and has a muffled voice and marked inspiratory stridor. She is maintaining SpO₂ at 96% on high-flow oxygen. What is the most appropriate airway management strategy in the retrieval setting?

options.A: Attempt direct laryngoscopy and orotracheal intubation immediately
options.B: Administer IV dexamethasone and observe for improvement before intubation
options.C: Maintain a calm environment, administer nebulised adrenaline, and transfer urgently without intubation
options.D: Perform a controlled gas induction in theatre at the receiving hospital if possible; prepare for surgical airway
options.E: Administer IV antibiotics and transfer immediately without airway intervention

explanation:
Epiglottitis is a life-threatening airway emergency — the airway is at imminent risk of complete obstruction, particularly with any agitation or distress. The gold standard management (endorsed by APLS, RCPCH, and the Oxford Specialist Handbook) is: controlled inhalational induction of anaesthesia in theatre with an ENT surgeon and senior anaesthetist present, with a surgical airway immediately available. This should occur at the receiving hospital if the patient is stable enough to transfer safely. The specific dangers in epiglottitis are: (1) airway instrumentation before adequate anaesthesia can precipitate complete obstruction (laryngospasm); (2) the child maintaining a compensatory posture — any disturbance (including attempting IV access or laying supine) can precipitate acute decompensation. In the retrieval context: keep the child calm, allow preferred position (sitting upright), avoid any distressing procedures, maintain supplemental oxygen by whatever means tolerated (blow-by oxygen is acceptable), have a surgical airway kit immediately available, and transfer expeditiously. Option A is dangerous — awake laryngoscopy risks precipitating total obstruction. Option B introduces delay. Option C is the closest to correct transfer management, but nebulised adrenaline (option C) is not standard for epiglottitis and IV antibiotics should be given. Option E is correct in including antibiotics but "without any airway consideration" is incomplete and dangerous.
________________________________________

text: A 19-year-old male with a GCS of 6 following a high-speed motorcycle collision has a CT head showing bilateral diffuse axonal injury with no surgically correctable lesion. He is intubated and ventilated. His ICP monitor shows ICP of 32 mmHg. His MAP is 78 mmHg. What is his cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) and how should it be managed?

options.A: CPP = 110 mmHg — normal, no intervention required
options.B: CPP = 46 mmHg — below the target of ≥60 mmHg; increase MAP with vasopressors
options.C: CPP = 46 mmHg — below target; reduce ICP with hyperventilation
options.D: His ICP monitor shows ICP of 32 mmHg. His MAP is 78 mmHg. What is his cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) and how should it be managed? A. CPP = 110 mmHg — normal, no intervention required B. CPP = 46 mmHg — below the target of ≥60 mmHg; increase MAP with vasopressors C. CPP = 46 mmHg — below target; reduce ICP with hyperventilation D. CPP = 32 mmHg — critically low; administer mannitol 1g/kg immediately
options.E: CPP = 78 mmHg — above target; reduce MAP to 65 mmHg

explanation:
Cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) = MAP − ICP. In this case: CPP = 78 − 32 = 46 mmHg. The Brain Trauma Foundation (BTF) guidelines (4th edition) and NICE CG176 recommend maintaining CPP ≥60 mmHg in TBI to ensure adequate cerebral blood flow. A CPP of 46 mmHg is critically low and risks cerebral ischaemia. The management of CPP involves two levers: (1) raising MAP (using vasopressors — noradrenaline is first-line), and/or (2) lowering ICP. In this scenario, raising MAP is the appropriate first step — option B is correct. Routine hyperventilation to lower PaCO₂ (option C) is not recommended for ongoing ICP management — it causes cerebral vasoconstriction and ischaemia, and any benefit is short-lived (15–30 minutes); it is only used as a temporising emergency measure (PaCO₂ target <3.5 kPa briefly) for acute herniation. Mannitol (option D) addresses ICP, not MAP, and at 1g/kg the CPP calculation remains unchanged by this answer choice. Option A and E reflect calculation errors. During retrieval, continuous invasive arterial blood pressure monitoring and ICP monitoring (where in situ) are essential to titrate vasopressor therapy and maintain CPP ≥60 mmHg.
________________________________________

text: A 55-year-old woman is retrieved following emergency craniotomy and clot evacuation for a right-sided acute subdural haematom

options.A: She is intubated on a propofol infusion. The neurosurgeon has asked the retrieval team to maintain a specific glucose range during transfer. What is the recommended blood glucose target in TBI to prevent secondary brain injury? A. 4.0–6.0 mmol/L (tight glycaemic control)
options.D: Allow free-running glucose — the brain benefits from hyperglycaemia post-injury
options.E: 4.0–8.0 mmol/L targeting the lower end

explanation:
Glycaemic management in TBI is critically important. Both hypoglycaemia and hyperglycaemia cause secondary brain injury: hypoglycaemia deprives the brain of metabolic substrate; hyperglycaemia causes osmotic shifts, worsens cerebral oedema, and is independently associated with worse outcomes in TBI. NICE CG176 and the Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines recommend avoiding both hypoglycaemia (<4 mmol/L) and hyperglycaemia (>10–12 mmol/L), with a target blood glucose of approximately 6–10 mmol/L. Tight glycaemic control (targeting 4–6 mmol/L, option A) was assessed in the NICE-SUGAR trial and found to increase mortality in critically ill patients due to hypoglycaemia risk — it is no longer recommended. Hyperglycaemia targets (options C and D) are contraindicated. Option E includes too low a lower bound. In practice during retrieval, point-of-care glucose monitoring every 30–60 minutes is recommended in ventilated TBI patients, with correction of hypoglycaemia using 10–20% dextrose and correction of significant hyperglycaemia with insulin infusion per local protocol.
________________________________________

text: A patient with a severe TBI (GCS 5T, bilateral fixed dilated pupils) develops acute deterioration en route — HR drops to 42 bpm and BP rises to 210/115 mmHg. Which eponymous response is this, and what does it indicate?

options.A: Bezold-Jarisch reflex — cardiac ischaemia from cerebral oedema
options.B: Cushing's response — critically raised ICP with imminent brainstem herniation
options.C: Bainbridge reflex — hypovolaemia-induced sympathetic response
options.D: Monro-Kellie doctrine — equilibrium between intracranial components
options.E: Cheyne-Stokes response — central respiratory pattern from cortical dysfunction

explanation:
The Cushing's response (or Cushing's triad) is the combination of hypertension (rising systolic BP), bradycardia (reflex), and irregular/slow respirations that occurs in response to critically raised ICP threatening brainstem herniation. It represents a last-ditch autonomic response to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure as ICP approaches MAP. The presence of this response in a patient with already-fixed dilated pupils (indicating uncal herniation with CN III compression) signifies an extreme emergency requiring immediate intervention. The Monro-Kellie doctrine (option D) describes the principle that the skull is a fixed compartment and ICP rises as any component increases — it is a principle, not a response. The Bezold-Jarisch reflex (option A) involves vagal bradycardia from cardiac irritation. The Bainbridge reflex (option C) involves heart rate increase with venous return. Cheyne-Stokes breathing (option E) is a breathing pattern, not a haemodynamic response. Immediate management in retrieval: emergency hyperventilation (PaCO₂ target 3.5–4.0 kPa briefly), IV mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg or hypertonic saline (3% NaCl 3–5 ml/kg), head of bed 30°, ensure adequate MAP, and contact neurosurgical centre for emergency escalation of management plan.
________________________________________

text: A 44-year-old man is retrieved following a road traffic collision with confirmed C5 fracture-dislocation and complete motor deficit below this level. During transfer, his BP falls to 70/40 mmHg and his HR is 42 bpm. His breathing is adequate. What is the most likely diagnosis and first-line treatment?

options.A: Hypovolaemic shock from occult haemorrhage — IV fluid resuscitation
options.B: Neurogenic shock from loss of sympathetic tone — vasopressors (noradrenaline) and IV fluids
options.C: Tension pneumothorax from rib injury — bilateral thoracostomy
options.D: Cardiogenic shock from myocardial contusion — dobutamine infusion
options.E: What is the most likely diagnosis and first-line treatment? A. Hypovolaemic shock from occult haemorrhage — IV fluid resuscitation B. Neurogenic shock from loss of sympathetic tone — vasopressors (noradrenaline) and IV fluids C. Tension pneumothorax from rib injury — bilateral thoracostomy D. Cardiogenic shock from myocardial contusion — dobutamine infusion E. Vasovagal episode — Trendelenburg positioning and IV fluids

explanation:
Neurogenic shock is a distributive shock state that occurs following spinal cord injuries at T6 and above (particularly cervical injuries), resulting from loss of descending sympathetic tone. The hallmark is the combination of: hypotension (due to vasodilation from loss of sympathetic vasoconstriction) AND bradycardia (from unopposed vagal tone, as the cardiac accelerator fibres from T1–T4 are also lost). This is the key distinguishing feature from hypovolaemic shock, which causes reflex tachycardia. Management: IV fluids (cautiously, as the vasodilation is the primary problem, not volume depletion) plus vasopressors — noradrenaline is first-line as it provides both alpha-1 vasoconstriction and some beta-1 inotropic support. Atropine may be needed for symptomatic bradycardia. It is critical not to over-resuscitate with fluids alone, which risks pulmonary oedema without addressing the vascular tone problem. Option A would be the correct answer for hypovolaemic shock but is inconsistent with bradycardia. Options C, D, and E are inconsistent with the clinical picture. In the retrieval context, invasive monitoring (arterial line, urinary catheter for urine output) is essential during vasopressor titration. Target MAP ≥85–90 mmHg for the first 7 days per current spinal cord injury guidelines.
________________________________________

text: A patient with suspected pelvic fracture following a pedestrian-versus-car collision is being retrieved from scene to a major trauma centre. She is haemodynamically unstable with an open-book pelvic fracture pattern on clinical examination. Which device should be applied before transfer?

options.A: Long spinal board with leg straps to immobilise the pelvis
options.B: Pelvic binder (e.g. SAM Pelvic Sling or T-POD) applied at the level of the greater trochanters
options.C: External fixator applied by the retrieval team
options.D: MAST (Military Anti-Shock Trousers)
options.E: She is haemodynamically unstable with an open-book pelvic fracture pattern on clinical examination. Which device should be applied before transfer? A. Long spinal board with leg straps to immobilise the pelvis B. Pelvic binder (e.g. SAM Pelvic Sling or T-POD) applied at the level of the greater trochanters C. External fixator applied by the retrieval team D. MAST (Military Anti-Shock Trousers) E. Pelvic binder applied at the level of the iliac crests

explanation:
An open-book pelvic fracture (anteroposterior compression injury) disrupts the pelvic ring, causing external rotation and separation of the pubic symphysis. This dramatically increases pelvic volume, allowing massive haemorrhage from disrupted pelvic venous plexuses and arterial branches. A circumferential pelvic binder (CPB) reduces this volume by internally rotating the femoral heads, compressing the pelvis and tamponading haemorrhage. The correct positioning is at the level of the greater trochanters — NOT the iliac crests (option E). Positioning at the iliac crests is incorrect and can distract rather than compress the posterior ring. This is explicitly stated in JRCALC guidelines, ATLS (10th edition), and the NICE NG39 Major Trauma guidelines. SAM Pelvic Sling and T-POD are commonly used devices in the UK and Ireland. External fixators (option C) are surgical procedures not performed by retrieval teams in the field. Long spinal boards (option A) do not provide circumferential pelvic compression. MAST trousers (option D) are no longer recommended. A CPB should remain in place throughout transfer and must not be removed for assessment or imaging without senior trauma surgery input.
________________________________________

text: A retrieval team is asked to transfer a patient with suspected viral haemorrhagic fever (VHF) — possible Ebola virus disease — from an isolation facility to a national high-consequence infectious disease (HCID) unit. What is the minimum level of personal protective equipment (PPE) required for the retrieval team?

options.A: Surgical mask, apron, and non-sterile gloves
options.B: FFP3 respirator, full-face visor, fluid-resistant gown, double gloves
options.C: Appropriate HCID-designated PPE including powered air-purifying respirator (PAPR), full-body impermeable suit, double gloves, and boot covers — as specified by national HCID guidance
options.D: Standard aerosol precautions: FFP2 respirator and disposable gown
options.E: PPE is determined by the clinical team at the receiving HCID unit and is not the retrieval team's responsibility

explanation:
Viral haemorrhagic fevers (including Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, and Crimean-Congo haemorrhagic fever) are classified as High Consequence Infectious Diseases (HCIDs) in the UK and Ireland. The management of HCID patients, including retrieval and transfer, follows strict national protocols issued by Public Health England (PHE)/UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) and the Health Protection Surveillance Centre (HPSC) in Ireland. For Category A HCIDs (including Ebola), PPE requirements are significantly above standard infection control measures and typically include: Powered Air-Purifying Respirator (PAPR) or equivalent full respiratory protection, full-body impermeable coverall, double or triple gloves, boot covers, and full donning/doffing protocols with a trained observer. Standard FFP3 with gown (option B) may be appropriate for other infectious diseases but is insufficient for Category A HCIDs. JRCALC and UK ambulance services have specific HCID PPE protocols, and retrieval teams should liaise with their regional HCID coordinator and public health authorities before deployment. Option E is incorrect — the retrieval team has full responsibility for their own PPE compliance.
________________________________________

text: You are retrieving a patient from a rural hospital with suspected open pulmonary tuberculosis. The patient is coughing and requires oxygen. Which precautions are most important during the retrieval?

options.A: Contact precautions only — gown and gloves
options.B: Droplet precautions — surgical mask for the patient and FFP2 mask for staff
options.C: Airborne precautions — FFP3 respirators for all staff, surgical mask on the patient, negative pressure environment where possible
options.D: Full HCID PPE as for viral haemorrhagic fever
options.E: Standard precautions are adequate as TB is not transmitted by air in ambulances

explanation:
Mycobacterium tuberculosis is transmitted by airborne droplet nuclei (particles <5 microns that remain suspended in air for extended periods). Airborne precautions are therefore required: FFP3 (or N99/N100 equivalent) respirators for all clinical staff, a surgical mask on the patient to reduce droplet generation, and a negative pressure environment where possible. In the ambulance/retrieval setting, a true negative pressure vehicle is rarely available — the practical equivalent is maximising ventilation (opening windows, activating air exchange systems) and minimising recirculation of cabin air. UK guidance from NICE NG33 (Tuberculosis), PHE, and the British Infection Association specifies FFP3 as the standard for TB airborne protection in clinical settings. Option B (droplet precautions with surgical mask) is insufficient for TB — surgical masks do not filter the small particles responsible for TB transmission. Option D (full HCID PPE) significantly overstates requirements. Option E is incorrect — enclosed transport vehicles are a recognised high-risk environment for TB transmission. After the mission, the vehicle should be ventilated and decontaminated, and the retrieval team may need to undergo occupational health follow-up depending on exposure duration and patient smear status.
________________________________________

text: A patient being retrieved has confirmed COVID-19 with severe pneumonia requiring high-flow nasal oxygen at 60 L/min. They are not intubated. Which statement most accurately reflects the infection control considerations during transfer?

options.A: High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) does not generate aerosols and requires only droplet precautions
options.B: HFNO is an aerosol-generating procedure (AGP); airborne precautions including FFP3 respirators are required
options.C: The patient should be intubated before transfer as HFNO is contraindicated in the retrieval setting
options.D: Which statement most accurately reflects the infection control considerations during transfer? A. High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) does not generate aerosols and requires only droplet precautions B. HFNO is an aerosol-generating procedure (AGP); airborne precautions including FFP3 respirators are required C. The patient should be intubated before transfer as HFNO is contraindicated in the retrieval setting D. Standard surgical masks for staff are adequate as the patient is wearing a mask
options.E: Airborne precautions are only required for patients requiring intubation or bronchoscopy

explanation:
High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) is classified as an aerosol-generating procedure (AGP) — it generates high-velocity airflow through the nasopharynx, creating respiratory aerosols. During the COVID-19 pandemic, this classification was confirmed by the UK Infection Prevention and Control Cell, Public Health England, and NICE guidance (NG191). AGPs require airborne precautions: FFP3 (or equivalent) respirators for all clinical staff in the vicinity. This applies throughout the transfer. The Resuscitation Council UK and FICM COVID-19 guidance extended this to all patients with confirmed or suspected COVID-19 undergoing AGPs. Option A is incorrect — HFNO is explicitly classified as an AGP. Option C reflects a dilemma that exists in practice (intubation vs. HFNO for safe transfer) but is not the correct answer to this specific question about infection control. In the retrieval context, if HFNO is continued during transfer, all team members must use FFP3. Cabin ventilation should be maximised. Option E is incorrect — the AGP classification is not limited to intubation/bronchoscopy.
________________________________________

text: A 35-year-old woman with 38% TBSA burns (20% full thickness) following a house fire has a hoarse voice, inspiratory stridor, and carbonaceous sputum. She is maintaining SpO₂ at 93% on 15L O₂ via non-rebreather mask. How should her airway be managed before transfer?

options.A: Observe and transfer urgently — intubation should be performed at the burns unit
options.B: Trial of nebulised adrenaline and steroids before considering intubation
options.C: RSI and intubation with a cuffed ETT before transfer — use a smaller tube than usual and prepare for difficult airway
options.D: Tracheostomy should be performed immediately by the referring surgical team
options.E: Apply high-flow nasal oxygen and transfer immediately

explanation:
Inhalation injury with evolving airway oedema is one of the most time-critical airway emergencies in burns medicine. The airway must be secured BEFORE transfer. The clinical signs — hoarse voice, stridor, carbonaceous sputum — indicate supraglottic oedema that will progressively worsen over the next few hours due to inflammatory oedema. Crucially, as oedema progresses, what begins as a manageable airway becomes impossible to intubate. The DAS difficult airway guidelines, British Burn Association (BBA) standards, and the Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine all recommend early, elective, controlled intubation before oedema progresses. Preparation for difficult airway is essential: supraglottic oedema can make laryngoscopy challenging; a slightly smaller ETT size is recommended (e.g. 7.0 for an average adult female rather than 7.5–8.0); video laryngoscopy is preferred; a surgical airway kit must be immediately available. A SpO₂ of 93% on 15L O₂ in a burns patient may underestimate true hypoxia due to carboxyhaemoglobin (co-oximetry is needed). Nebulised adrenaline (option B) is an inappropriate delay. Waiting for the burns unit (option A) risks a "cannot intubate, cannot oxygenate" scenario during transfer. Tracheostomy (option D) is not a primary intervention for airway protection in this acute scenario.
________________________________________

text: Using the Rule of Nines for a standard adult, a patient has burns covering the entire right arm, the anterior trunk, and the perineum. What percentage total body surface area (TBSA) is affected?
explanation:
The Rule of Nines allocates body surface area as: Head and neck = 9%; Each arm (including hand) = 9%; Anterior trunk = 18%; Posterior trunk = 18%; Each leg = 18% (9% thigh + 9% lower leg/foot); Perineum = 1%. Calculation: Right arm = 9%, Anterior trunk = 18%, Perineum = 1% → Total = 28%. Wait — that gives 28%, option A. Let me re-examine: Right arm = 9%, Anterior trunk = 18%, Perineum = 1% = 28%. Option A (28%) would be correct by standard Rule of Nines. 
________________________________________

text: A child aged 3 years sustains burns to both legs and the posterior trunk. Using the Lund and Browder chart rather than the Rule of Nines, which statement best explains why this approach is preferred for paediatric burns assessment?

options.A: The Lund and Browder chart is faster to use in the prehospital setting
options.B: The Lund and Browder chart accounts for age-related differences in body proportions, particularly the relatively larger head and smaller legs in children compared with adults
options.C: The Rule of Nines overestimates burns in children, leading to excessive fluid resuscitation
options.D: The Lund and Browder chart is required by NICE guidelines for all burns assessments regardless of age
options.E: The Lund and Browder chart is only used for full-thickness burns

explanation:
The Rule of Nines is calibrated for adult body proportions. In children, the head represents proportionally more body surface area (approximately 18% in infants, decreasing with age) and the legs proportionally less, compared with adults. Using the Rule of Nines in a child would significantly underestimate burns involving the head and overestimate those involving the legs, leading to inaccurate fluid resuscitation calculations. The Lund and Browder chart corrects for this by adjusting the percentage allocated to each body region according to the child's age. The British Burn Association and NICE guidance recommend the Lund and Browder chart for burns assessment in all age groups, but it is particularly important in children. Option A is incorrect — Lund and Browder is more complex and time-consuming. Option C incorrectly characterises the direction of error. Option D partially reflects guidance but misses the key age-related justification. Option E is incorrect — the chart is used for all depths of burns in %TBSA estimation.
________________________________________

text: A 22-year-old male with known bipolar disorder is to be retrieved from a psychiatric facility to a general hospital for medical assessment of a drug overdose. He is agitated, refuses to cooperate, and is threatening to leave. He has capacity to make decisions. Which of the following legal frameworks is most appropriate to authorise transport against his wishes in Ireland?

options.A: Section 4 of the Mental Health Act 2001 (emergency admission order)
options.B: Common law doctrine of necessity
options.C: Guardianship of Persons of Unsound Mind legislation
options.D: The Mental Health Act 2001 (Ireland) provides no mechanism for involuntary medical treatment
options.E: He is agitated, refuses to cooperate, and is threatening to leave. He has capacity to make decisions. Which of the following legal frameworks is most appropriate to authorise transport against his wishes in Ireland? A. Section 4 of the Mental Health Act 2001 (emergency admission order) B. Common law doctrine of necessity C. Guardianship of Persons of Unsound Mind legislation D. The Mental Health Act 2001 (Ireland) provides no mechanism for involuntary medical treatment E. The individual cannot be transported against their wishes if they have capacity

explanation:
In Ireland, involuntary detention and transport of a person with a mental disorder who poses a risk to themselves or others is governed by the Mental Health Act 2001. Section 4 provides for emergency admission: a registered medical practitioner may recommend involuntary admission if they believe the person has a mental disorder and that failure to detain would cause serious harm to the person or to others. This enables involuntary transport to an approved centre. The Mental Capacity Act 2015 principles (as adopted in Irish law via the Assisted Decision-Making (Capacity) Act 2015) draw a distinction between capacity and best interests — a person may have impaired capacity due to acute psychiatric illness even if they appear superficially articulate. The distinction between mental capacity law and mental health law is important: mental health legislation allows involuntary detention for treatment of mental disorder; capacity legislation guides decisions about medical treatment. The common law doctrine of necessity (option B) may apply in life-threatening emergencies but is a less robust framework for planned inter-facility transfer. Option D is incorrect — the Act does provide mechanisms. Option E is incorrect because capacity may be impaired by the mental disorder itself, and even where present, mental health law may override autonomy. This question reflects the Irish legislative framework specifically, which differs from the Mental Health Act 1983 (England and Wales).
________________________________________

text: During retrieval of a patient with acute behavioural disturbance who has been sedated with IM ketamine, which of the following complications is most important to monitor for and anticipate?

options.A: Malignant hyperthermia
options.B: Laryngospasm, hypersalivation, and emergence agitation
options.C: Prolonged neuromuscular blockade
options.D: Serotonin syndrome
options.E: Severe hypertension refractory to treatment

explanation:
Ketamine is a dissociative anaesthetic with an excellent safety profile for sedation of agitated patients. However, specific side effects require monitoring during retrieval. The most clinically important are: (1) Laryngospasm — rare but can occur, particularly with excessive secretions; (2) Hypersalivation — ketamine is a potent sialagogue, increasing secretion production which can precipitate laryngospasm; a glycopyrrolate or atropine pre-treatment is recommended by some services to reduce this risk; (3) Emergence agitation/dysphoria — as ketamine wears off, patients may experience vivid hallucinations, agitation, or confusion; co-administration of low-dose benzodiazepine (e.g. midazolam 0.05 mg/kg) significantly reduces emergence phenomena. Monitoring requirements: continuous SpO₂, capnography if available, HR, BP, and readiness to manage airway including RSI if laryngospasm occurs. Malignant hyperthermia (option A) is associated with volatile anaesthetic agents and succinylcholine, not ketamine. Neuromuscular blockade (option C) — ketamine does not cause this. Serotonin syndrome (option D) is not a ketamine-specific concern. Severe refractory hypertension (option E) can occur with ketamine (it is sympathomimetic) but is rarely clinically significant in the context of excited delirium where baseline sympathetic tone is already very high. The Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine specifically identifies laryngospasm and hypersalivation as key ketamine risks.
________________________________________

text: A patient with acute behavioural disturbance is being transferred by helicopter. He is sedated but increasingly restless. The retrieval physician is considering additional sedation. What specific consideration unique to the aviation environment must be factored into this decision?

options.A: Altitude decreases drug bioavailability, requiring higher doses
options.B: The patient must not receive any further sedation once airborne — only oral medication is permitted in flight
options.C: Aviation safety regulations require the patient to be restrained; any sedation affecting airway reflexes may complicate rapid egress in an emergency
options.D: Noise levels in the aircraft prevent monitoring of respiratory rate, making sedation unsafe
options.E: Reduced cabin temperature at altitude potentiates sedative effects

explanation:
The aviation environment introduces a unique regulatory and safety consideration when managing sedated patients. Aviation law requires that emergency egress (rapid exit from the aircraft in the event of crash, ditching, or emergency landing) must be possible for all occupants, including patients. Physical restraint systems used for agitated patients must allow emergency release. More specifically, deep sedation that compromises airway reflexes (e.g. loss of gag reflex, inability to swallow secretions) creates a risk that cannot be adequately managed in an aircraft — especially in a crash scenario where suction and airway equipment may not be immediately accessible. The retrieval physician must balance: (1) the medical requirement to sedate; (2) the aviation safety principle of maintaining the ability to manage the patient's airway in an emergency; (3) the minimal restraint principle (legal obligation to minimise harm to the patient). The Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine dedicates specific guidance to this tension: "The retrieval clinician must balance the transport safety requirement with the legal/ethical medical duty of care." Options A and E are pharmacologically incorrect for relevant clinical altitudes. Option B is incorrect — there is no prohibition on IV/IM medication in flight. Option D partially reflects a real challenge but does not represent the most clinically important specific consideration.
________________________________________

text: A patient on an intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) requires retrieval from a district general hospital to a cardiac surgery centre 90 minutes away. Which of the following statements about IABP retrieval is correct?

options.A: The IABP must be converted to manual pumping for the duration of the road transfer
options.B: Helium-inflated IABPs are safe for air transport as helium is inert and does not expand at altitude
options.C: The IABP console requires mains electrical power throughout transfer — battery operation is not possible
options.D: At altitude, helium within the IABP balloon expands according to Boyle's Law, potentially causing over-inflation — the inflation volume must be adjusted or altitude minimised
options.E: IABPs should be removed before any transfer as they cannot function during movement

explanation:
Intra-aortic balloon pumps use helium as the inflation gas because it is inert, has low density, and diffuses rapidly through tissues if the balloon ruptures. However, according to Boyle's Law (pressure × volume = constant at constant temperature), as altitude increases and atmospheric pressure decreases, the volume of a fixed amount of gas increases. This means the helium within the IABP balloon will expand at altitude, potentially causing over-inflation (which could damage the balloon or cause aortic trauma) and may alter the timing and effectiveness of counterpulsation. For air transport of IABP patients, the standard recommendations (from the IABP literature and Oxford Specialist Handbook of Retrieval Medicine) include: reduce balloon fill volume before ascent, use pressurised aircraft or fly at the lowest safe altitude, monitor IABP augmentation waveform continuously, and liaise with the IABP manufacturer guidance. Option B incorrectly states helium is safe without modification — inertness doesn't prevent expansion. Option C is incorrect — modern IABP consoles have substantial battery backup. Option E is incorrect — IABPs continue to function during transport. Option A (manual pumping) would eliminate counterpulsation benefit and is only used as a temporary measure when automatic triggering fails.
________________________________________

text: A patient with cardiogenic shock post-STEMI has an IABP in situ. The retrieval physician notices the IABP augmentation waveform on the monitor shows the augmented diastolic pressure is lower than the patient's systolic pressure. What is the most likely explanation?

options.A: The balloon is inflating too late in the cardiac cycle (late inflation)
options.B: The balloon is correctly timed — this waveform pattern is normal
options.C: The IABP is operating in 1:2 assist mode, which reduces augmented diastolic pressure
options.D: The balloon is deflating too early
options.E: What is the most likely explanation? A. The balloon is inflating too late in the cardiac cycle (late inflation) B. The balloon is correctly timed — this waveform pattern is normal C. The IABP is operating in 1:2 assist mode, which reduces augmented diastolic pressure D. The balloon is deflating too early E. The IABP trigger is set to ECG mode rather than pressure mode

explanation:
Optimal IABP function requires precise timing. The balloon should inflate at the dicrotic notch of the arterial waveform (the point of aortic valve closure) and deflate just before the next systole. The primary goal of inflation is to augment diastolic pressure (improving coronary and peripheral perfusion); the primary goal of deflation is to reduce afterload (reducing cardiac workload). Normal, optimally timed IABP: augmented diastolic pressure > unassisted systolic pressure. When augmented diastolic pressure is lower than systolic pressure, the most common cause is late inflation — the balloon inflates after the dicrotic notch, missing the early diastolic period when maximal augmentation occurs, resulting in suboptimal diastolic augmentation. Other timing errors include: early inflation (inflates before dicrotic notch — competes with ventricular ejection, dangerous); early deflation (balloon deflates before optimal diastolic runoff); late deflation (balloon deflates too late, increasing afterload). IABP timing assessment and troubleshooting is a core competency tested in the DipRTM syllabus (subdomain 1.14.7–1.14.8). Option C (1:2 mode) does produce lower average augmentation per cycle but this is intentional and does not explain the relationship described.
________________________________________

text: Which of the following most accurately describes the primary indication for venoarterial ECMO (VA-ECMO) in the retrieval context?

options.A: Severe hypoxic respiratory failure not responsive to conventional ventilation
options.B: Cardiogenic shock refractory to vasopressors and IABP support, or cardiac arrest refractory to CPR (eCPR)
options.C: Post-cardiac arrest cooling for targeted temperature management
options.D: Septic shock with refractory hypotension
options.E: ARDS with PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio <100 mmHg

explanation:
ECMO is classified into two main configurations: (1) Venovenous ECMO (VV-ECMO): drains venous blood, oxygenates it, and returns it to the venous circulation — used for respiratory failure (e.g. severe ARDS) as it provides gas exchange but no cardiac support; (2) Venoarterial ECMO (VA-ECMO): drains venous blood and returns oxygenated blood to the arterial circulation — provides both cardiac and respiratory support; indicated for cardiogenic shock (refractory to conventional therapy), and eCPR (extracorporeal CPR during refractory cardiac arrest). The distinction is important: option A and option E describe indications for VV-ECMO, not VA-ECMO. Option C (TTM) can be achieved on ECMO but is not itself an indication. Option D (septic shock) may occasionally have VA-ECMO as a bridge but is not the primary indication. In the DipRTM context, the retrieval physician should understand: ECMO retrieval involves a specialist team, requires meticulous planning, and carries specific risks during transport including circuit failure, oxygenator failure, cannula dislodgement, and air embolism. Retrieval of patients on VA-ECMO (either mobile ECMO — where ECMO is initiated at the referring centre before transfer, or "ECMO to the patient" and then transfer) is increasing and is explicitly examined in subdomain 1.15.
________________________________________

text: A 48-year-old woman with fulminant myocarditis is on VA-ECMO following refractory cardiogenic shock. She is being retrieved to an ECMO/transplant centre 200 km away. The ECMO flow is 4.2 L/min. During transfer, the oxygenator begins to show progressively declining oxygen transfer efficiency (falling post-oxygenator PO₂). What is the most likely cause and immediate action?

options.A: Oxygenator clotting (thrombosis) — increase heparin infusion and prepare backup oxygenator
options.B: Air embolism — clamp the circuit and prepare for emergency recirculation
options.C: Cannula malposition — reposition under imaging guidance
options.D: ECMO flow too high — reduce flow to 2 L/min
options.E: The patient is improving — wean ECMO

explanation:
Progressive decline in oxygenator efficiency (falling post-membrane PO₂, rising transmembrane pressure gradient, visible clot within the oxygenator) is the hallmark of oxygenator thrombosis — one of the most serious ECMO circuit complications. It represents a failure of the gas exchange component and can lead to life-threatening hypoxia in a patient who is dependent on the ECMO for oxygenation and cardiac support. Management: (1) Optimise anticoagulation — check ACT (target typically 180–200 seconds on VA-ECMO) and increase heparin infusion; (2) Notify the destination ECMO centre immediately for guidance; (3) If a backup oxygenator is available, prepare for circuit change (a planned, controlled procedure by trained ECMO specialists); (4) Maximise native cardiac and respiratory support if possible. This scenario underscores why ECMO retrieval requires specialist teams, dedicated ECMO equipment, and pre-planned contingencies. Air embolism (option B) presents differently (sudden complete loss of flow, visible air bubbles). Cannula malposition (option C) affects flow, not oxygenation efficiency specifically. Reducing flow (option D) would worsen end-organ perfusion. Option E is not supported by a failing oxygenator. The Oxford Specialist Handbook and ELSO (Extracorporeal Life Support Organisation) guidelines provide the framework for ECMO transport risk management.
________________________________________

text: Which of the following scenarios best describes the concept of 'mobile ECMO' in the retrieval context, and what is the key advantage over 'conventional retrieval' of a patient in refractory cardiogenic shock?

options.A: Mobile ECMO refers to an ECMO console small enough to fit in a standard ambulance — the advantage is reduced equipment weight
options.B: Mobile ECMO refers to a specialist team that travels to the patient, initiates ECMO at the referring centre, and then transfers the patient on ECMO — the advantage is that haemodynamic support is established before the risks of transfer
options.C: Mobile ECMO refers to transferring the patient rapidly to an ECMO centre without any pre-transfer intervention — the advantage is time saving
options.D: Mobile ECMO is only applicable to VV-ECMO for respiratory failure
options.E: Mobile ECMO refers to using a helicopter to transfer an ECMO patient — the advantage is speed

explanation:
Mobile ECMO (or 'retrieval ECMO') represents an evolution in critical care retrieval: rather than the conventional model of transporting an unstable patient to an ECMO centre (with all the risks of transferring a patient in refractory cardiogenic shock or arrest), the ECMO team travels to the patient and initiates extracorporeal support at the referring centre. Once the patient is stable on ECMO with adequate cardiac output and oxygenation, the retrieval (on ECMO) is performed. This approach dramatically reduces the haemodynamic instability during transfer and has been shown to improve outcomes in centres with active mobile ECMO programmes (ECMO Australia, UK centres). The key advantage is that the patient is transferred once they are haemodynamically supported, rather than in extremis. In Ireland, NAS CCRS liaises with ECMO centres (primarily the Mater University Hospital, Dublin) for ECMO retrievals. This concept is directly addressed in DipRTM subdomain 1.15.6 ("relative risks of moving patients before or after implementation of ECLS") and 1.15.7 ("operational and logistical considerations"). Options A, C, D, and E all mischaracterise the concept in important ways.
DRTM Question Bank — Batch 3
Domain 2: Environmental | Subdomains 2.1–2.7
30 Single Best Answer Questions
